summary:
Child Abuse & Neglect Recognition

Child abuse and neglect are a tragic reality of prehospital care. As an EMS provider, you are a mandated reporter — you are legally required to report suspected abuse to the appropriate authorities. You do NOT need proof; you only need reasonable suspicion. Understanding the patterns and indicators of abuse, neglect, and Munchausen by proxy is essential for protecting vulnerable children. This section covers the recognition, documentation, and reporting of child maltreatment.

Legal Obligation: Reporting suspected child abuse is NOT optional. Every EMS provider in every state is a mandated reporter. Failure to report suspected abuse can result in criminal charges, loss of certification, and civil liability. You do NOT need the child's or parent's consent to report, and you are immune from civil liability when reporting in good faith.

Physical Abuse Indicators

Physical abuse is the intentional infliction of physical injury upon a child. It accounts for approximately 18% of all reported child maltreatment cases. The hallmark of physical abuse is injury that is inconsistent with the reported mechanism or the child's developmental capabilities. Key indicators include:

Patterned Bruising

Bruises from abuse have distinctive shapes and patterns that reflect the object used to inflict them. Unlike accidental bruises, which typically occur over bony prominences (shins, knees, elbows, forehead), abusive bruises appear on soft, protected areas:

  • Handprint bruises — Clear outline of fingers and palm on the face, buttocks, or back. The spaces between fingers may be spared (negative imprint).
  • Belt/strap marks — Linear, loop-shaped, or rectangular bruises corresponding to the shape of the object. A belt buckle leaves a distinctive pattern.
  • Pinch or grip marks — Oval-shaped bruises on the upper arms, chest, or neck where a child has been grabbed forcefully.
  • Bruises in various stages of healing — Multiple bruises of different colors (red/purple = fresh, blue/brown = several days old, yellow/green = 5-7+ days old) indicate repeated episodes.
  • Bruises on non-mobile infants — Any bruise on an infant who is not yet crawling or walking is highly suspicious for abuse. Accidental bruising is rare in pre-ambulatory children.
Bruise ColorApproximate AgeClinical Significance
Red/ Purple/ BlueFresh (0-2 days)Recent injury — deoxygenated blood in tissues
Dark blue/ Purple/ Brown2-5 daysHemoglobin breakdown begins
Green/ Brown5-7 daysBiliverdin pigment appears
Yellow/ Brown7-10+ daysBilirubin pigment — bruise is resolving
Multiple colors presentMixed agesRED FLAG — indicates repeated, ongoing abuse
Fractures

Certain fracture patterns are highly suspicious for abuse, particularly in children who are not yet walking or who cannot explain the mechanism:

  • Spiral fractures of the humerus or femur — Caused by a twisting force (e.g., grabbing and twisting a child's arm or leg). In a non-ambulatory infant, a femoral fracture is virtually never accidental.
  • Metaphyseal corner (bucket handle) fractures — A classic abusive fracture caused by violently shaking or pulling an infant's extremities. The metaphysis (growth plate area) is torn away from the bone shaft. These fractures are virtually pathognomonic for abuse.
  • Posterior rib fractures — Caused by squeezing a child's chest (anterior-to-posterior compression). The posterior rib heads are levered against the transverse processes of the vertebrae. Rib fractures in infants are highly specific for abuse.
  • Multiple fractures in different stages of healing — Indicates repeated traumatic episodes over time.
  • Skull fractures — Complex, depressed, or stellate (star-shaped) fractures; fractures that cross suture lines; fractures in the parietal or occipital region from a fall is unlikely in young infants.
Burns

Burns from abuse have characteristic patterns that differentiate them from accidental burns:

  • Immersion burns (stocking/glove pattern) — When a child's hands or feet are forcibly held in hot water, the burn has a sharp line of demarcation (waterline) with uniform depth. Accidental immersion (e.g., a child stepping into a hot bath) produces an irregular, splash-pattern burn with varying depth.
  • Cigarette burns — Round, 1-cm diameter burns with a deep central crater and sharp margins. Typically found on the palms, soles, or genitalia. Unlike impetigo or chickenpox, cigarette burns are deep, uniform, and have rolled edges.
  • Contact burns — Burns shaped like the object used (iron, curling iron, stove coil, radiator). Look for the pattern within the burn.
  • Forced immersion of buttocks and perineum — "Dunking" burn where the child's buttocks and perineum are held in hot water, sparing the flexural creases (the skin in the creases is protected when the legs are held together).
  • Scald burns from hot liquids — Splash patterns (irregular, asymmetric) are more consistent with accidental spills. Immersion patterns (symmetric, clear waterline) are suspicious for intentional injury.
Pattern Recognition: Accidental burns are typically superficial and irregular, with splash marks and varying depths. Abusive burns are typically deep and uniform, with sharp borders and specific shapes matching the object or immersion pattern.
Head Trauma — Shaken Baby Syndrome (Abusive Head Trauma)

Shaken baby syndrome (SBS) — now more accurately called abusive head trauma (AHT) — is caused by violent shaking of an infant, with or without impact. The rapid acceleration-deceleration forces cause the brain to move within the skull, shearing bridging veins and causing direct brain injury. SBS is the leading cause of fatal child abuse in the United States, with the highest incidence in infants under 6 months of age.

The classic triad of findings in abusive head trauma:

  • Retinal hemorrhages — Bleeding at the back of the eye caused by the acceleration-deceleration forces pulling the vitreous body from the retina. Seen in 50-80% of AHT cases. Bilateral and multi-layered retinal hemorrhages are highly specific for SBS.
  • Subdural hematoma (SDH) — Bleeding between the dura and the brain surface caused by tearing of the bridging veins. The SDH in SBS is typically thin, bilateral, and spread over the cerebral convexities.
  • Cerebral edema — Diffuse brain swelling from the primary mechanical injury and secondary hypoxic-ischemic injury.

The shaken baby may present with a wide range of severity: from nonspecific symptoms (irritability, vomiting, poor feeding, lethargy) to seizures, apnea, or complete cardiovascular collapse. External signs of trauma are often absent — the absence of bruising or external injury does NOT rule out SBS. The perpetrator is most commonly the father or mother's boyfriend, followed by the mother, and the most common trigger is the infant crying inconsolably.

Abdominal Trauma

The abdomen is the second most common site of fatal abuse after the head. Abdominal injuries result from blunt force — a punch, kick, or compression. Due to a child's smaller body surface area and less developed abdominal musculature, the force transmits more deeply. Common injuries include:

  • Duodenal hematoma — The duodenum is compressed against the spine, causing intramural hematoma. Presents with vomiting and abdominal pain.
  • Liver or spleen laceration — Caused by direct blow to the upper abdomen. May present with shock, abdominal distension, and guarding.
  • Pancreatic injury — The pancreas is compressed against the spine. Later presents with pseudocyst formation.
  • Hollow viscus perforation — The jejunum is most commonly torn at its fixation point near the ligament of Treitz.

Any abdominal injury in a child with no history of major trauma (MVC, fall from height) should raise concern for abuse.

Neglect Indicators

Child neglect is the most common form of maltreatment (approximately 60% of all cases). It is the failure of a caregiver to provide for a child's basic physical, emotional, medical, or educational needs. Neglect is often chronic and may be more difficult to identify than physical abuse:

  • Failure to thrive (FTT) — Significant growth delay (weight, height, and/or head circumference below the 5th percentile or falling off the growth curve). May present with emaciation, listlessness, and developmental delay. Differentiate organic (medical) vs. non-organic (environmental/neglect) causes.
  • Poor hygiene — Unwashed child, matted hair, severe diaper rash or dermatitis, dirty/ragged clothing inappropriate for weather, strong odor, dental decay (nursing bottle caries).
  • Unattended medical needs — Untreated infections, wounds, or fractures; missing scheduled immunizations; unmanaged chronic conditions (asthma, diabetes, seizures) with frequent ED visits for exacerbations.
  • Developmental delay — The child is not meeting age-appropriate developmental milestones (motor, language, social). May be related to lack of stimulation, affection, or safe environment.
  • Supervision neglect — Young children left unsupervised, repeatedly found in dangerous situations, or left with inadequate or intoxicated caregivers.
  • Nutritional neglect — Consistent hunger, stealing or hoarding food, eating from trash, or severely underweight.
  • Educational neglect — Chronic truancy, failure to enroll in school, or refusal to address special educational needs.

Sexual Abuse

Sexual abuse involves any sexual act with a child by an adult or older child. It affects children of all ages, socioeconomic backgrounds, and cultures. Most perpetrators are known to the child (family members, family friends, caretakers). Physical findings are often absent or subtle — the absence of genital trauma does NOT rule out sexual abuse:

  • Genital or anal trauma — Bruising, lacerations, abrasions, or bleeding of the external genitalia, hymen, perineum, or anus. Healing scars, anal fissures, or hymenal transections may be seen in chronic abuse.
  • Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in a prepubertal child — The presence of any STI (gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, HIV, HPV, trichomonas) in a child beyond the neonatal period is diagnostic of sexual abuse until proven otherwise. HPV (genital warts) in a child is highly suspicious.
  • Pregnancy — Any pregnancy in a pre-adolescent or young adolescent requires investigation.
  • Behavioral changes — Age-inappropriate sexual knowledge or behavior (e.g., a 5-year-old acting out sexual acts with dolls or other children), regressive behaviors (thumb-sucking, bedwetting), sleep disturbances, nightmares, unexplained fear of a specific person or place, sudden school refusal, running away, or self-harm.
  • Nonspecific somatic complaints — Chronic abdominal pain, headaches, dysuria, or vaginal discharge without identifiable medical cause.

Munchausen by Proxy (Factitious Disorder Imposed on Another)

Munchausen by proxy (MBP) is a form of abuse in which a caregiver — usually the mother — deliberately fabricates, exaggerates, or induces illness in a child to gain medical attention, sympathy, and the emotional rewards of the "sick role" by proxy. It is one of the most complex and dangerous forms of child abuse to identify because the caregiver often appears deeply concerned and knowledgeable about medical issues:

  • Red flags: The child has a history of multiple unexplained illnesses, repeated hospitalizations, multiple surgeries, reported allergies or intolerances to multiple medications, and symptoms that occur only in the caregiver's presence.
  • Common methods: Induced apnea (smothering), induced vomiting (ipecac or salt poisoning), contamination of IV lines with feces or bacteria, insulin or laxative administration, suffocation, and fabricated fevers (warming thermometers).
  • Assessment: Note that the caregiver often refuses to leave the child's side, is overly knowledgeable about medical terminology, has a healthcare background, and seems to enjoy the relationship with medical staff. Symptoms may not match any known disease pattern, and the child may improve in the hospital only to deteriorate again shortly after discharge.
  • EMS provider role: Document the history as reported, including all details about previous medical evaluations. Be objective — do not accuse. Your documentation may be critical in identifying a pattern of unnecessary medical interventions.
NREMT Key Point: You do NOT need proof of abuse to report — only reasonable suspicion. If you suspect abuse, your legal duty is to report to Child Protective Services (CPS) or law enforcement. Do NOT confront the parents or accuse anyone. Your job is to document objectively, protect the child from immediate danger, and report. If the child is in immediate danger, do not leave them in that environment — involve law enforcement.

EMS Provider Reporting Obligations

As a mandated reporter, you must adhere to the following principles:

  • Reasonable suspicion is enough — You are not a detective. You do not need conclusive evidence. If the injury pattern, history, or presentation raises concern, report it.
  • Report immediately — Most states require immediate reporting (by phone) followed by a written report within 24-48 hours. Know your state's CPS hotline number. If you do not know the number, report to law enforcement or the hospital social worker.
  • Do NOT accuse parents — Never say "I think you abused your child." This is not your role. Instead, document what you see and report your objective findings to the appropriate authorities. Accusations can jeopardize the investigation and create liability.
  • Report objectively — State only what you observed (bruises, burns, statements) and what the child or caregiver said. Avoid speculation about who caused the injuries.
  • Preserve evidence — Do not wash wounds or change the child's clothing unnecessarily. If clothing must be removed for care, cut around evidence (e.g., cut around a tear in clothing rather than through it). Document any evidence handling.
  • Involve law enforcement if in immediate danger — If the child would be at risk if left at the scene, do not leave them. Contact law enforcement to ensure safety.
  • Immunity from liability — Mandated reporters are protected by law from civil or criminal liability when reporting in good faith. This protection applies even if the report turns out to be unfounded.

Documentation Guidelines

Documentation in abuse cases is critical — it may become key evidence in criminal or child protection proceedings:

  • Objective findings only — Describe what you see, not what you conclude. Instead of "abusive burns," write "well-demarcated, full-thickness burns on both feet with sharp upper border at the ankle, sparing the flexural creases." Instead of "neglect," write "child is wearing only a diaper in 40°F weather, appears thin with visible ribs, and has severe diaper rash with open sores."
  • Avoid speculation — Do not write "mother seems angry" or "father appears drunk." Write "mother is yelling" or "father has slurred speech and the smell of alcohol on his breath."
  • Document quotes verbatim — If the child says something about the injury, write the exact words in quotation marks. If the caregiver's explanation does not match the injury, document both the explanation and the injury description. Example: "Caregiver states the child 'fell off the couch,' but the child has a spiral fracture of the femur, which is inconsistent with a fall from a low height."
  • Document everyone present — Note who was at the scene, who provided the history, and the child's behavior and demeanor.
  • Photograph injuries — If permitted by your service protocol, take photographs of injuries before transport. Include a ruler or coin for scale and the patient's identifying information (but not in the same frame as identifiable body parts in sensitive areas).
  • Document to whom you reported — Note the name, agency, and time of your report to CPS or law enforcement.
Documentation Example — Good vs. Poor:
Poor: "Child was abused by mother. Burns look intentional." (speculative, accusatory)
Good: "12-month-old female presents with well-demarcated, full-thickness burns on bilateral hands extending 2 cm above the wrist with a sharp horizontal border. Mother states the child 'touched the stove,' but the burns are symmetric, uniform depth, and have clear waterlines — consistent with forced immersion injury. Child's developmental stage is non-ambulatory (not crawling or walking). Reported to CPS at 1430 hours. Case number: #2025-XXXXX."

quiz.q: An EMS provider is called to a home for a 6-month-old infant who is irritable and vomiting. The mother states the infant 'rolled off the couch.' The infant has no visible external injuries but is lethargic and has a bulging fontanelle. What condition should the EMS provider suspect?
options: Gastroenteritis
Abusive head trauma (shaken baby syndrome)
A simple fall — infants are resilient
Meningitis

quiz.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding an EMS provider's legal obligation to report suspected child abuse?
options: The EMS provider must have conclusive proof of abuse before reporting
Reporting is required only if the parent admits to abuse
The EMS provider needs only reasonable suspicion — reporting is mandatory
Reporting is optional and at the EMS provider's discretion

quiz.q: A 3-year-old child presents with multiple bruises in various stages of healing, including a handprint-shaped bruise on the face. The parent states the child 'falls a lot.' What is the EMS provider's BEST action?
options: Accept the parent's explanation since toddlers are clumsy
Document the injuries as suspicious, report to CPS/law enforcement, and transport for evaluation
Confront the parent about the inconsistency between the story and injuries
Refuse transport since the child appears stable

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a typical indicator of child neglect?
options: Failure to thrive with growth below the 5th percentile
A clean child with appropriate clothing for the weather
Untreated infections and missing immunizations
Consistent hunger and hoarding food

quiz.q: A child presents with uniform, full-thickness burns on both feet with a sharp, horizontal border just above the ankles. The parent states the child 'stepped in hot water in the bathtub.' Which of the following is TRUE?
options: This is consistent with an accidental splash burn
The sharp border and uniform depth suggest forced immersion — this is suspicious for abuse
The burn pattern is not relevant — only the parent's explanation matters
Burns cannot be abusive because hot water burns are always accidental

quiz.q: HARD: An EMS provider assesses a 4-year-old female with a history of multiple hospitalizations for 'breathing problems,' 'seizures,' and 'allergic reactions.' The mother is highly knowledgeable about medical terminology and refuses to leave the child's side. The child's symptoms have only occurred when the mother was present, and multiple specialists have been unable to find an organic cause. What condition should the EMS provider consider?
options: Munchausen by proxy (factitious disorder imposed on another)
A genuinely complex medical child
Primary seizure disorder
Parental anxiety disorder

quiz.q: HARD: An EMS provider is transporting a 2-year-old with suspected physical abuse. The child's father is in the back of the ambulance and keeps asking, 'What did you write in your report? Are you going to call the police?' The father becomes increasingly agitated. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Reassure the father that no report is being made to keep the scene calm
Politely explain that the EMS provider is required by law to report suspected abuse, ask the father to ride in the front with the driver, and contact law enforcement if safety is a concern
Stop the ambulance and refuse to transport until the father calms down
Hand the PCR to the father to show him what was written

flashcards.question: What are the three classic findings of shaken baby syndrome (abusive head trauma)?
flashcards.answer: The triad: (1) Retinal hemorrhages — bilateral, multi-layered bleeding at the back of the eye; (2) Subdural hematoma — bleeding over the cerebral convexities from torn bridging veins; (3) Cerebral edema — diffuse brain swelling.

flashcards.question: What bruise patterns are characteristic of physical abuse in children?
flashcards.answer: Bruises on soft/protected areas (face, buttocks, back, abdomen), handprint bruises, belt/strap marks, pinch/grip marks, bruises in various stages of healing (multiple colors), and any bruise on a non-mobile infant.

flashcards.question: What is the EMS provider's legal obligation when they suspect child abuse?
flashcards.answer: As a mandated reporter, the EMS provider must report immediately to Child Protective Services or law enforcement. You DO NOT need proof — only reasonable suspicion. Do NOT accuse parents. Document objectively. You are immune from liability when reporting in good faith.

flashcards.question: What fracture patterns are highly suspicious for child abuse?
flashcards.answer: Spiral fractures (femur/humerus) in non-ambulatory children, metaphyseal corner (bucket handle) fractures, posterior rib fractures, and multiple fractures in different stages of healing.

flashcards.question: What is Munchausen by proxy (factitious disorder imposed on another)?
flashcards.answer: A form of abuse where a caregiver deliberately fabricates, exaggerates, or induces illness in a child to gain attention. Red flags include symptoms only in the caregiver's presence, multiple unexplained hospitalizations, and a caregiver who appears overly knowledgeable and involved.

flashcards.question: How should an EMS provider document suspected abuse in the PCR?
flashcards.answer: Use objective findings only (describe, do not diagnose). Document the child's exact words in quotes. Record the caregiver's explanation alongside the injury description. Note to whom the report was made and the case number. Avoid speculation or accusations.

flashcards.question: What are indicators of child neglect?
flashcards.answer: Failure to thrive (growth delay), poor hygiene (unwashed, inappropriate clothing, severe diaper rash), unattended medical needs, developmental delay, supervision neglect (left unsupervised), nutritional neglect, and educational neglect (chronic truancy).

flashcards.question: What burn patterns suggest intentional abuse rather than accidental injury?
flashcards.answer: Immersion burns (stocking/glove pattern with sharp waterline), cigarette burns (round, deep, uniform 1 cm), contact burns shaped like the object, and forced immersion of buttocks/perineum. Accidental burns are irregular with splash patterns and varying depth.

critical.scenario: You are dispatched to a residence for a 'fussy infant' call. Upon arrival, you find a 4-month-old male in the care of his mother's boyfriend. The boyfriend states the baby 'just won't stop crying' and has been 'spitting up everything.' The infant is lethargic, has a weak cry, and you note a bulging anterior fontanelle. There are no visible bruises on the infant's body. The boyfriend is evasive about what happened and changes his story from 'he just started doing this' to 'I had to shake him a little to get him to stop crying.' The mother is not home. The boyfriend says he doesn't want the baby to go to the hospital and offers to call the mother to take the baby to the pediatrician tomorrow instead.
critical.question: Given the infant's presentation (lethargy, bulging fontanelle, weak cry) and the caregiver's changing story and admission of shaking, what is the EMS provider's priority?
options: Respect the boyfriend's request and document the mother's contact information for follow-up — the baby seems stable for now

Recognize this as suspected abusive head trauma: the infant is in immediate danger. Explain that the baby needs emergency evaluation. Transport immediately, contact law enforcement en route (the boyfriend admitted shaking), and document the exact statements made. Do NOT leave the infant with this caregiver
Wait for the mother to return before making any decisions about transport
Call CPS before transporting to get permission to take the baby to the hospital

critical.scenario: You respond to a 9-1-1 call for a 6-year-old boy with a 'burn injury.' The mother explains that the child was 'playing with matches' and burned his own hand. The child is quiet and avoids eye contact with you. As you examine the burn, you note a well-circumscribed, round, deep burn approximately 1 cm in diameter on the palm of his right hand with a central crater. There are three additional identical burns on his left forearm. The burns appear to be in different stages of healing — some are fresh with blisters, while others are healed scars. The mother's explanation about matches does not account for the pattern or distribution of the burns on both hands and forearm, and the child does not confirm or deny the mother's story.
critical.question: What is the most likely explanation for these burns and how should the EMS provider proceed?
options: The child was indeed playing with matches — document the mother's story and provide burn care

The burns appear consistent with cigarette burns (round, 1 cm, deep, uniform, multiple stages of healing, on the palm where sensation is high — a classic torture pattern). The mother's story does not match the injuries. Document objective findings, provide burn care, transport, and report suspected abuse to the hospital and CPS. Do NOT confront the mother
These are likely caused by a skin condition — the child should see a dermatologist
The child is old enough to explain what happened — insist on getting the child's story before making any decisions

critical.scenario: You and your partner are called to an elementary school for a 7-year-old girl who 'fell in gym class' and injured her arm. The school nurse meets you and tells you privately that she is concerned about this child. Over the past six months, the child has been seen in the nurse's office for multiple injuries — each time with a different explanation. Today, the child has obvious deformity of her left forearm. The nurse shows you her file, which documents: a black eye three months ago ('ran into a door'), facial bruising two months ago ('sister hit her with a toy'), and now a forearm fracture. The nurse says the child has also been coming to school hungry, wearing the same clothes repeatedly, and has missed 15 days of school this year. When you examine the child, she is withdrawn and does not make eye contact. She flinches when you touch her arm and looks toward the door as if she wants to escape. When you ask what happened, she whispers, 'I fell. I fall a lot.' Her teacher, who is standing nearby, mouths to you, 'Something is not right at home.'
critical.question: Given the pattern of multiple injuries, the concerning social indicators (hunger, poor hygiene, truancy, withdrawn behavior), and the school staff's concerns, what is the EMS provider's obligation?
options: Treat the fracture, transport to the hospital, but do not get involved with the social concerns — it is not your role

Treat the fracture, transport the child. The EMS provider has reasonable suspicion of both physical abuse AND neglect based on the pattern of multiple injuries in various stages of healing, the indicators of nutritional neglect, and the child's behavior. Report to CPS and law enforcement before leaving the scene. Provide a thorough PCR documenting each injury, the child's statements verbatim, the nurse's documented concerns, and the teacher's observation. Let the school staff know the child is being transported so they can cooperate with the investigation
Call the parents to ask if they can explain the injuries before involving CPS
Tell the school nurse that since the child is at school, it is the school's responsibility to report — not yours
